title: Psalms 149
subtitle: Sing to the LORD a New Song

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “Let Israel Rejoice in Their Maker”

heb.paragraph: "Sing to the LORD a new song (shîr ḥādāsh), his praise in the assembly of his faithful people" (v.1). The penultimate hallelujah. Let Israel rejoice in their Maker (v.2). "Let them praise his name with dancing and make music to him with timbrel and harp" (v.3). Dancing and instruments: the body joins the voice. "For the LORD takes delight in his people; he crowns the humble with victory" (v.4). God’s delight is in his people — and he crowns the humble.

notes.note: "Sing a new song. Let Israel rejoice in their Maker; let the people of Zion be glad in their King." Maker and King: God creates Israel and rules Israel. Both roles produce joy.

"Praise his name with dancing, with timbrel and harp. The LORD takes delight in his people; he crowns the humble with victory." God’s delight: not the strong but the humble. The crown: not achievement but divine gift.

refs.note: The great multitude shouts hallelujah; the wedding of the Lamb has come.
Speak to one another with psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs.
Let the word of Christ dwell richly; sing with gratitude in your hearts.

hist.context: Psalm 149 calls for a 'new song' celebrating YHWH's delight in his people. The assembly of the faithful (hasidim) rejoices with dancing, tambourine, and lyre. This is embodied worship—joy expressed physically. The psalm reflects festival celebration, perhaps eschatological anticipation of final victory. God 'crowns the humble with victory'—salvation as royal honor bestowed.

sections.header: Verses 5–9 — “A Double-Edged Sword in Their Hands”

heb.paragraph: "Let the high praises of God be in their mouths and a double-edged sword (ḥereb) in their hands" (v.6). The most militant hallelujah psalm. Praise AND sword. Worship AND warfare. The faithful bind kings with chains and nobles with iron shackles (v.8). "This is the honour of all his faithful people" (v.9). The honour: to execute God’s written judgment. Hebrews 4:12: "the word of God is sharper than any double-edged sword."

notes.note: "Let his faithful people rejoice in this honour and sing for joy on their beds. Let the high praises of God be in their mouths and a double-edged sword in their hands." Praise on the lips + sword in the hand. Worship and warfare are simultaneous, not sequential.

"To carry out the sentence written against them — this is the honour of all his faithful people. Hallelujah!" The honour of the saints: executing God’s judgment. Not vengeance but justice. Hallelujah.

refs.note: The overcomer will rule nations with an iron scepter—authority to execute judgment.
The saints will judge the world; we will judge angels.
The Lord Jesus will punish those who do not know God—vengeance fulfilled.

hist.context: The psalm's second half surprises: praise on lips, double-edged swords in hands—to execute vengeance on nations and bind their kings. This militant imagery troubled interpreters. Some read it as holy war ideology; others as eschatological: the saints participate in God's final judgment. The 'sentence written against them' points to prophetic decrees (Isa 13-23, Jer 46-51). Israel's worship anticipates divine justice.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: " Maker and King: God creates Israel and rules Israel…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVto carry out the sentence written against them— this is the glory of all his faithful people. Praise the Lord.ESVto execute on them the judgment written! This is honor for all his godly ones. Praise the LORD!NIVLet his faithful people rejoice in this honor and sing for joy on their beds.ESVLet the godly exult in glory; let them sing for joy on their beds.

rec.text: ""Sing a new song. Let Israel rejoice in their Maker; let the people of Zion be glad in their King." Maker and King: God creates Israel and rules Israel. Both roles produce joy."

rows.label: Praise and Sword — 149:5–9
rows.text: "High praises in their mouths and a DOUBLE-EDGED SWORD in their hands." Worship as warfare. Hallelujah.

hebtext.note: "Sing to the LORD a new song (shîr ḥādāsh), his praise in the assembly of his faithful people" (v.1). The penultimate hallelujah. Let Israel rejoice in their Maker (v.2). "Let them praise his name with dancing and make music to him with timbrel and harp" (v.3). Dancing and instruments: the body joins the voice. "For the LORD takes delight in his people; he crowns the humble with victory" (v.4). God’s delight is in his people — and he crowns the humble.

"Let the high praises of God be in their mouths and a double-edged sword (ḥereb) in their hands" (v.6). The most militant hallelujah psalm. Praise AND sword. Worship AND warfare. The faithful bind kings with chains and nobles with iron shackles (v.8). "This is the honour of all his faithful people" (v.9). The honour: to execute God’s written judgment. Hebrews 4:12: "the word of God is sharper than any double-edged sword."

themes.note: Worship at 9: praise + sword. Judgment: the honour of executing God’s written sentence. The militant hallelujah.
